examples.title: Pseudocode: Linked Stack and Queue Operations
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating stack (LIFO) and queue (FIFO) operations using linked lists with self-referential structures
examples.code:
ALGORITHM StackNode
// Self-referential structure for stack/queue node
STRUCT Node
 data // The stored value
 next // Pointer to next node
END STRUCT

// ==================== STACK OPERATIONS ====================

ALGORITHM Push(top, value)
// Add element to top of stack (O(1))
INPUT: top pointer, value to push
OUTPUT: Updated top pointer

BEGIN
 newNode ← ALLOCATE(Node) // malloc(sizeof(Node))
 IF newNode = NULL THEN
 PRINT "Memory allocation failed"
 RETURN top
 END IF
 
 newNode.data ← value // Store data
 newNode.next ← top // Point to current top
 top ← newNode // Update top to new node
 RETURN top
END

ALGORITHM Pop(top)
// Remove and return top element (O(1))
INPUT: top pointer
OUTPUT: Popped value, Updated top pointer

BEGIN
 IF top = NULL THEN
 PRINT "Stack Underflow"
 RETURN NULL, -1 // Error indicator
 END IF
 
 temp ← top // Store current top
 value ← temp.data // Save data to return
 top ← top.next // Move top to next node
 FREE(temp) // Prevent memory leak
 RETURN top, value
END

ALGORITHM Peek(top)
// Return top element without removing (O(1))
INPUT: top pointer
OUTPUT: Top element value

BEGIN
 IF top = NULL THEN
 PRINT "Stack is empty"
 RETURN NULL
 END IF
 RETURN top.data
END

// ==================== QUEUE OPERATIONS ====================

ALGORITHM Enqueue(front, rear, value)
// Add element to rear of queue (O(1))
INPUT: front pointer, rear pointer, value to enqueue
OUTPUT: Updated front and rear pointers

BEGIN
 newNode ← ALLOCATE(Node)
 IF newNode = NULL THEN
 PRINT "Memory allocation failed"
 RETURN front, rear
 END IF
 
 newNode.data ← value
 newNode.next ← NULL // End of list
 
 IF rear = NULL THEN // Queue is empty
 front ← newNode
 rear ← newNode
 ELSE
 rear.next ← newNode // Link to new node
 rear ← newNode // Update rear
 END IF
 
 RETURN front, rear
END

ALGORITHM Dequeue(front, rear)
// Remove element from front of queue (O(1))
INPUT: front pointer, rear pointer
OUTPUT: Dequeued value, Updated front and rear pointers

BEGIN
 IF front = NULL THEN
 PRINT "Queue Underflow"
 RETURN front, rear, -1
 END IF
 
 temp ← front // Store front node
 value ← temp.data // Save data
 front ← front.next // Move front to next
 
 IF front = NULL THEN // Queue became empty
 rear ← NULL
 END IF
 
 FREE(temp)
 RETURN front, rear, value
END

// ==================== DISPLAY OPERATIONS ====================

ALGORITHM DisplayStack(top)
// Display stack from top to bottom
INPUT: top pointer
BEGIN
 IF top = NULL THEN
 PRINT "Stack is empty"
 RETURN
 END IF
 
 temp ← top
 PRINT "Stack (top → bottom): "
 WHILE temp ≠ NULL DO
 PRINT temp.data
 temp ← temp.next
 END WHILE
 PRINT "NULL"
END

ALGORITHM DisplayQueue(front)
// Display queue from front to rear
INPUT: front pointer
BEGIN
 IF front = NULL THEN
 PRINT "Queue is empty"
 RETURN
 END IF
 
 temp ← front
 PRINT "Queue (front → rear): "
 WHILE temp ≠ NULL DO
 PRINT temp.data
 temp ← temp.next
 END WHILE
 PRINT "NULL"
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the core operations for both stacks and queues using linked lists. For stacks, push and pop occur at the head (O(1)), maintaining LIFO order. For queues, enqueue happens at the rear and dequeue at the front (both O(1)), maintaining FIFO order. Key points: (1) Always check for NULL after malloc, (2) Update pointers correctly to maintain list integrity, (3) Free memory after pop/dequeue to prevent leaks, (4) Handle edge cases when list becomes empty.

examples.title: Python: Linked Stack and Queue Implementation
examples.description: Complete Python implementation of stack and queue using linked lists with all operations
examples.code:
class Node:
 """Self-referential structure for linked stack/queue"""
 def __init__(self, data):
 self.data = data
 self.next = None


class LinkedStack:
 """Stack implementation using linked list - LIFO behavior"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.top = None # Head of the list is the top
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """Check if stack is empty - O(1)"""
 return self.top is None
 
 def push(self, value):
 """Push element onto stack - O(1)"""
 new_node = Node(value)
 new_node.next = self.top # New node points to current top
 self.top = new_node # Update top to new node
 print(f"Pushed {value} to stack")
 
 def pop(self):
 """Pop element from stack - O(1)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Stack Underflow! Cannot pop from empty stack.")
 return None
 
 temp = self.top # Store current top
 value = temp.data # Save data to return
 self.top = self.top.next # Move top to next node
 # In Python, garbage collector handles memory
 print(f"Popped {value} from stack")
 return value
 
 def peek(self):
 """Return top element without removing - O(1)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Stack is empty!")
 return None
 return self.top.data
 
 def display(self):
 """Display stack contents from top to bottom - O(n)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Stack is empty!")
 return
 
 print("Stack (top → bottom): ", end="")
 temp = self.top
 elements = []
 while temp:
 elements.append(str(temp.data))
 temp = temp.next
 print(" -> ".join(elements) + " -> NULL")


class LinkedQueue:
 """Queue implementation using linked list - FIFO behavior"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.front = None # Head - deletion point
 self.rear = None # Tail - insertion point
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """Check if queue is empty - O(1)"""
 return self.front is None
 
 def enqueue(self, value):
 """Add element to rear of queue - O(1)"""
 new_node = Node(value)
 new_node.next = None # End of list
 
 if self.rear is None: # Queue is empty
 self.front = new_node
 self.rear = new_node
 else:
 self.rear.next = new_node # Link to new node
 self.rear = new_node # Update rear
 
 print(f"Enqueued {value} to queue")
 
 def dequeue(self):
 """Remove element from front of queue - O(1)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue Underflow! Cannot dequeue from empty queue.")
 return None
 
 temp = self.front # Store front node
 value = temp.data # Save data to return
 self.front = self.front.next # Move front to next
 
 if self.front is None: # Queue became empty
 self.rear = None
 
 print(f"Dequeued {value} from queue")
 return value
 
 def get_front(self):
 """Return front element without removing - O(1)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue is empty!")
 return None
 return self.front.data
 
 def get_rear(self):
 """Return rear element without removing - O(1)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue is empty!")
 return None
 return self.rear.data
 
 def display(self):
 """Display queue contents from front to rear - O(n)"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue is empty!")
 return
 
 print("Queue (front → rear): ", end="")
 temp = self.front
 elements = []
 while temp:
 elements.append(str(temp.data))
 temp = temp.next
 print(" -> ".join(elements) + " -> NULL")


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
 print("=" * 50)
 print("LINKED STACK DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 stack = LinkedStack()
 
 # Test stack operations
 stack.push(10)
 stack.push(20)
 stack.push(30)
 stack.display()
 
 print(f"\nTop element (peek): {stack.peek()}")
 print(f"Is empty: {stack.is_empty()}")
 
 stack.pop()
 stack.pop()
 stack.display()
 
 stack.pop()
 stack.pop() # Underflow
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 50)
 print("LINKED QUEUE DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 queue = LinkedQueue()
 
 # Test queue operations
 queue.enqueue(10)
 queue.enqueue(20)
 queue.enqueue(30)
 queue.display()
 
 print(f"\nFront element: {queue.get_front()}")
 print(f"Rear element: {queue.get_rear()}")
 
 queue.dequeue()
 queue.dequeue()
 queue.display()
 
 queue.enqueue(40)
 queue.enqueue(50)
 queue.display()
 
 queue.dequeue()
 queue.dequeue()
 queue.dequeue() # Underflow
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates both stack and queue using linked lists. The LinkedStack class uses a single pointer (top) with push/pop at the head for O(1) operations. The LinkedQueue class maintains both front and rear pointers - enqueue adds at rear, dequeue removes from front. Key implementation details: (1) Node class is self-referential with data and next pointer, (2) Python's garbage collector handles memory cleanup (unlike C), (3) Edge cases handled: underflow checks, empty queue after dequeue updates both pointers.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of elements, plus O(1) auxiliary for operations

examples.title: C: Linked Stack and Queue Implementation
examples.description: Complete C implementation with proper memory management using malloc/free for linked stack and queue
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates both linked stack and queue with proper memory management. Key features: (1) Stack uses single 'top' pointer, operations at head for O(1), (2) Queue maintains 'front' and 'rear' pointers - enqueue at rear, dequeue at front, both O(1), (3) Memory management: malloc for allocation, free after pop/dequeue to prevent memory leaks (critical for university exams), (4) Edge cases: underflow checks, when queue becomes empty after dequeue, both front and rear set to NULL, (5) NULL checks after malloc to handle allocation failures. Time complexities: all core operations O(1), display O(n).
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing n elements, O(1) auxiliary space for operations
